metadata.summary: Wireframes are low-fidelity structural representations of a UI — boxes, labels, and annotations without colors, images, or final copy — used to validate layout, hierarchy, and interaction behavior before any visual design begins.

content.title: Wireframing
content.summary: Wireframes are low-fidelity structural representations of a UI — boxes, labels, and annotations without colors, images, or final copy — used to validate layout, hierarchy, and interaction behavior before any visual design begins. Jumping directly to high-fidelity design conflates structure decisions with aesthetic decisions, making both harder to evaluate. Wireframes keep feedback focused on "does this layout serve the user goal?" and allow cheap iteration before stakeholder attention locks in on visual details.

why.p: Jumping directly to high-fidelity design conflates structure decisions with aesthetic decisions, making both harder to evaluate. Wireframes keep feedback focused on "does this layout serve the user goal?" and allow cheap iteration before stakeholder attention locks in on visual details.

list.item: Translating a product spec or user story into a structural layout before visual design begins.
Rapid exploration of multiple layout alternatives for a new page or feature.
Generating annotated wireframe documentation from existing designs for developer handoff.
Producing content hierarchy maps to align stakeholders on structure before aesthetics.
Auditing existing screens for missing states (empty, error, loading) and documenting them.
After visual design has already been approved — retroactive wireframing is documentation theater.
For micro-interactions and animations — wireframes convey structure, not motion.
As a replacement for user research — wireframing answers "how to lay it out," not "what to build".
When the deliverable requires interactive prototype testing — proceed directly to interactive prototypes.
One-screen fixes or copy changes where layout is already established.

rule.statement: Wireframes show layout structure, content hierarchy, navigation, functionality, and interaction patterns. They exclude colors, typography styles, images, branding, and final copy.
rule.rationale: Mixing structural decisions with aesthetic decisions conflates two different types of feedback; stakeholders comment on the wrong thing and structure problems are missed.

rule.statement: Use only grayscale in wireframes; add no color except to distinguish interactive elements from static ones.
rule.rationale: Any color in a wireframe draws attention to aesthetics, not structure. Even "just one accent color" redirects stakeholder feedback away from layout.

rule.statement: Match fidelity to the decision being made: low-fidelity for concept exploration, medium for structure validation, high for pre-visual-design review.
rule.rationale: High-fidelity wireframes before structure is validated waste time on detail that will be discarded; low-fidelity after alignment is established fails to communicate the layout precisely enough.

rule.statement: Start with 3+ rough sketches before opening any tool — the tool commits you to a direction before you have explored enough.
rule.rationale: Tools impose structure (frames, grids, components) that constrains early ideation; pencil sketches are faster and cheaper to discard.

rule.statement: Use real content volume estimates, not Lorem Ipsum — "3 items" vs "247 items" produces wildly different layouts.
rule.rationale: Placeholder content hides information density problems; a card that looks clean with 3 items breaks with 247.

rule.statement: Annotate every element with: what it is, how it behaves, and what happens in each relevant state — behavior is the deliverable, not the box.
rule.rationale: Wireframes without behavior annotations are ambiguous; developers make implementation decisions that diverge from design intent.

rule.statement: For every interactive element and form, document all applicable states: Default, Hover, Focus, Active, Disabled, Loading, Error, Empty, Success.
rule.rationale: Missing states are the most common wireframing gap; developers implement default-only behavior and edge cases become bugs.

rule.statement: Create mobile wireframes as a separate deliverable in the same session — do not treat them as a footnote or an afterthought.
rule.rationale: Mobile layouts involve different content hierarchy decisions than desktop; they cannot be derived from desktop wireframes without explicit design work.

rule.statement: Share wireframes for feedback as questions ("Does this hierarchy match how users think?"), not statements ("Here's the design").
rule.rationale: Presenting wireframes as finished invites defensive reactions; framing them as hypotheses invites collaborative critique.

rule.statement: Never present a wireframe alongside a visual mockup in the same stakeholder review — the mockup always captures all attention.
rule.rationale: Aesthetic artifacts dominate mixed-fidelity reviews; structural feedback is lost when a high-fidelity image is in the room.

section.name: common-element-patterns
section.p: Navigation: [Logo] [Link] [Link] [Link] [CTA] — horizontal bar with logo left, nav center, primary action right.
Hero section: [Headline] / [Subtext] / [Primary CTA Button] — three-element stack, clear visual hierarchy by size.
Card: bordered rectangle with [Image area] / Title / Description / [Action] — four zones, top-to-bottom reading order.
Form: Label above [Input field] pairs, grouped in sections, with [Submit Button] at bottom — labels above inputs, not inline.
Footer: [Link groups] | [Social icons] | [Copyright and legal] — three horizontal zones.

antipattern.description: Adding color to wireframes — even "just one accent color" for the primary button.
antipattern.reason: Any color redirects stakeholder feedback from structure to aesthetics. A single colored button generates 20 minutes of color discussion in a review that should be about layout.

antipattern.description: Wireframing only the default state — no empty, error, or loading states documented.
antipattern.reason: Missing states are discovered during development; developers implement default-only behavior and edge cases become bugs. State coverage must be explicit in the wireframe deliverable.

antipattern.description: No annotations — wireframe boxes without any behavior documentation.
antipattern.reason: An unannotated wireframe is a Rorschach test. Every developer and stakeholder interprets element behavior differently; the disagreement surfaces in QA, not in review.

antipattern.description: Jumping to a tool before sketching — opening Figma as the first step.
antipattern.reason: Design tools impose grids, component libraries, and frame structures that commit the designer to a layout direction before sufficient exploration. Paper sketches are faster and cheaper to discard.

antipattern.description: Using Lorem Ipsum placeholder text instead of real content volume estimates.
antipattern.reason: Lorem Ipsum hides information density problems. A navigation that fits "Home / About / Products" breaks with "Enterprise Resource Planning Module Configuration".

antipattern.description: Creating desktop wireframes only and noting "mobile TBD."
antipattern.reason: Mobile layouts require different hierarchy decisions than desktop — they cannot be derived from desktop wireframes without explicit design work. Mobile is a separate deliverable, not a footnote.

section.name: fidelity-progression
section.p: Low-fidelity: boxes and lines, quick exploration, multiple concepts, pencil and paper or sticky notes. Cheap to discard. Use for early ideation before stakeholder alignment.
Medium-fidelity: basic content labels, rough layout, no visual design. Use for structure validation and early stakeholder review.
High-fidelity: detailed layout with all element positions, complete annotations, all states documented. Use as the final deliverable before visual design begins.

template.purpose: Full wireframe documentation: purpose, user goal, layout, annotations, states, interactions, responsive notes, open questions.
Component-level wireframe: purpose, variants, states, content guidelines, usage notes.

script.purpose: Bash script to list all pages and top-level frames in a Figma file via the Figma REST API.
